Code fragment: public final TableField<AuthorRecord, Integer> ID = createField(DSL.name("id"), SQLDataType.INTEGER.nullable(false), this, "");
Explanation: The primary key column is named just 'id', which is a generic name that doesn't convey any meaning about the table it belongs to. Consider renaming it to 'author_id' to make it more descriptive and avoid ambiguity in queries with joins.

Code fragment: public final TableField<BookRecord, Integer> ID = createField(DSL.name("id"), SQLDataType.INTEGER.nullable(false), this, "");
Explanation: The primary key column is generically named 'id' instead of a more descriptive name like 'book_id'. Consider renaming the column to 'book_id' to make it self-documenting and avoid ambiguity when joining tables.

Using `select()` without specifying columns fetches all columns from the AUTHOR table, which can retrieve unnecessary data and hurt performance. Explicitly list only the required columns, e.g., `db.select(AUTHOR.ID, AUTHOR.NAME)`.

Using `select()` without specifying columns fetches all columns from the BOOK table, which can retrieve unnecessary data and hurt performance. Explicitly list only the required columns, e.g., `db.select(BOOK_ID, BOOK_TITLE)`.
